title: Barrier crossing in a two-state system: Effect of bias and stochastic fields
authors: Sara Oliver-Bonafoux, Raúl Toral, Amitabha Chakrabarti
abstract: We study barrier crossing in a two-state system, namely the kinetic Ising model, in the presence of a weak bias field and spatially homogeneous, but time-dependent, Gaussian random fields. We find that the bias field determines the location of the dominant maxima of the probability distribution function of the magnetization, whereas the noise intensity controls their sharpness and stability of the distribution. A moderate stochastic field lowers the effective energy barrier and facilitates transitions between ordered states, while strong noise induces broad distributions and significant backflow, which reduces directional selectivity. Our results suggest that efficient barrier crossing requires a balanced combination of moderate stochastic driving and controlled bias.
summary: This paper examines how steady bias and random magnetic fields influence the rate of transitions between states in a kinetic Ising model. It finds that while moderate noise helps overcome energy barriers, excessive noise leads to a loss of directional control. The results highlight how stochastic driving can effectively minimize finite-size effects in the system's dynamics.
main_problem: Investigating how a combination of a weak steady bias field and time-dependent Gaussian random fields affects barrier crossing and switching dynamics in a two-state system.
main_result: Moderate stochastic fields lower the effective energy barrier and facilitate transitions, while strong noise induces backflow and reduces directional selectivity; additionally, stochastic driving suppresses finite-size effects.
method: Monte Carlo simulations using single-spin heat-bath dynamics on a 2D square lattice.
model_or_system: A kinetic Ising model on a 2D square lattice with periodic boundary conditions, subject to a Hamiltonian with ferromagnetic coupling, a constant bias field H, and a time-dependent Gaussian random field h_r(t).
key_observables: Probability distribution of magnetization P(m), time-averaged magnetization Q, and average first-passage time <tau>.
important_parameters: Temperature (T), noise intensity (D), bias field (H), and system size (L).
main_assumptions: The stochastic magnetic field is assumed to be spatially homogeneous (uniform across the lattice).
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows a phase diagram in the (D, T) plane; Fig 2 displays P(m) for various parameters; Fig 3 compares P(m) for different system sizes; Fig 4 shows <tau> vs D for various H and T; Fig 5 demonstrates suppression of finite-size effects; Fig 6 shows <tau> vs H.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the model and its phase diagram, explores the effect of noise and bias on the magnetization distribution, analyzes first-passage times and their dependence on noise and bias, and concludes with a discussion on system-size dependence and optimal transition conditions.
why_interesting: The study of noise-induced transitions and the modulation of energy barriers by stochastic and bias fields is highly relevant to understanding decoherence and switching dynamics in open quantum systems and driven many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Phase diagram of the model defined by Eq. (1) in the absence of a bias field (H = 0) in the (D, T) plane, where T is the temperature and 2D the vari- ance of the Gaussian random field. The sketches illus- trate the qualitative shape of the stationary magneti- zation distribution P(m) in the thermodynamic limit and the corresponding value of the time-averaged mag- netization Q in each region. The horizontal dashed line marks the critical temperature of the field-free Ising model, Tc ≈2.269. The solid line separates the broad- ferromagnetic and ferromagnetic phases. It is obtained numerically by identifying, for several values of D, the temperature below which no switching events are...

FIG. 2. Probability distribution of the magnetization, P(m), for a fixed bias field H = 0.05 and varying ran- dom field intensities (D = 0, 0.1, 0.5), and for two sys- tem sizes (L = 50, 200). Note that graphs in the same row share the same vertical scale, indicating that, ex- cept for the zero-variance case D = 0, the distribu- tions exhibit no significant dependence on the system size L. This behavior is further analyzed in Fig. 3. Results correspond to 106 measurements after a ther- malization period of 104 MCS. In the simulations, we take measurements of the magnetization over an obser- vation period of tobs = 106 MCS after a thermalization time t0 = 104 MCS. Histograms are constructed...

FIG. 3. Probability distribution of the magnetiza- tion, P(m), for a bias field H = 0.05 and temperature T = 2.0 for two system sizes (L = 50, 200) and two random field intensities: (a) D = 0.1 and (b) D = 0.5. These results confirm the lack of dependence on the system size observed in Fig. 2. In the numerical sim- ulations, the thermalization and measurement times have been set as in Fig. 2.

FIG. 4. Average first-passage time ⟨τ⟩from the or- dered state m = −1 to the barrier region m = 0, for a system of size L = 100, as a function of the random field intensity D, for different values of the bias field H and several temperatures. The averages ⟨τ⟩are com- puted from a sample of 103 first-passage times. Solid lines correspond to fits of Eq. (9) to the data. Note that all panels share the same vertical scale. In panel (a), vertical lines denote the critical noise intensity D0(T) separating the broad-ferromagnetic and ferro- magnetic phases for T = 0, 1, 2, with D0(T = 0) ≈0.6, D0(T = 1) ≈0.2 and D0(T = 2) ≈0.04 (see Fig. 1).

FIG. 5. Average first-passage time ⟨τ⟩as a function of stochastic field strength D for several temperatures and system sizes, in the absence of a bias field (H = 0). Note the absence of finite-size effects in the barrier- crossing times. Solid lines show fits of Eq. (9) to the data for L = 200. Vertical dashed lines indicate the approximate stochastic intensity separating the broad- ferromagnetic and ferromagnetic phases for T = 0, 1, 2 (from right to left), with the corresponding values re- ported in the caption of Fig. 4. All average times ⟨τ⟩ have been calculated from a sample of 103 first-passage times.
